chemicals.concentration_notes: It is widely considered the principal toxin in onions7 and is described as a "very poisonous" organosulfur compound that easily overwhelms a cat's antioxidant defenses
chemicals.description: It acts as a potent oxidant that causes a marked decrease in the activity of glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) in feline red blood cells2.... G6PD is essential for producing NADPH, which maintains glutathione in a reduced state67. Without it, glutathione levels drop, leading to an accumulation of hydrogen peroxide and lipid peroxidation56. This oxidative stress denatures the hemoglobin, for
chemicals.name: N-propyl disulfide (also referred to as Di-propyl-disulfide)

chemicals.concentration_notes: This is a highly active oxidizing compound that has been isolated specifically from boiled onions, proving that the toxic principles survive cooking14. It is also classified as "very poisonous"
chemicals.description: Similar to N-propyl disulfide, it reduces glutathione concentrations in erythrocytes25. It specifically increases methemoglobin concentrations and Heinz body counts in the blood, leading to subsequent oxidative cell damage and hemolytic anemia25. Concentration/Potency Notes: This is a highly active oxidizing compound that has been isolated specifically from boiled onions, proving that the toxic pr
chemicals.name: Sodium n-propylthiosulfate

chemicals.concentration_notes: It has historically been implicated as a primary cause of onion-induced hemolytic anemia, though more recent research has proposed that sodium n-propylthiosulfate and other newly discovered sulfur compounds are even more active oxidizers
chemicals.description: Like other organosulfur compounds in onions, it is readily absorbed through the gastrointestinal tract and metabolized into highly reactive oxidants that trigger oxidative hemolysis1.... Concentration/Potency Notes: It has historically been implicated as a primary cause of onion-induced hemolytic anemia, though more recent research has proposed that sodium n-propylthiosulfate and other newly disco
chemicals.name: Allyl propylisulfide

chemicals.concentration_notes: These compounds are responsible for the characteristic odor and flavor of onions910. Because they are present throughout the plant and survive processing, drying, and cooking, they make all forms of onions highly dangerous to cats
chemicals.description: These are the parent compounds sequestered inside the plant's cells. When the onion experiences mechanical trauma (such as being chewed, chopped, or cut), these non-toxic precursors instantly convert into the complex mixture of highly toxic sulfur-containing organic compounds (like the ones listed above)3.... Concentration/Potency Notes: These compounds are responsible for the characteristic odor
chemicals.name: Alk(en)yl cysteine sulfoxides (Precursors)

description: Onions (Allium cepa) are severely toxic to cats. All Allium species — including onions, garlic, chives, and leeks — contain organosulfur compounds that oxidise feline haemoglobin, forming Heinz bodies and triggering haemolytic anaemia. All forms are equally dangerous: raw, cooked, powdered, or dehydrated onions. Onion powder is especially hazardous due to its high concentration.
emergencyNote: 1. Oxygen Therapy and Blood Transfusions (Life-Saving Stabilization)
name: Onions
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: N-propyl disulfide and organosulfur compounds (alk(en)yl cysteine sulfoxides); all forms toxic
Mechanism: oxidative damage to haemoglobin creates Heinz bodies; the spleen destroys affected red blood cells, causing haemolytic anaemia
Symptoms: lethargy, weakness, pale or yellowish gums, rapid breathing, loss of appetite, reddish-brown urine; onset often delayed 3–5 days
Onion powder in baby food, broths, gravies, or prepared meals is a common hidden source of toxicity
Never share any onion-containing food with cats; contact your vet immediately if ingestion is suspected

symptoms.name: Vomiting, Diarrhea, Abdominal Pain, and Loss of Appetite
symptoms.notes: These are usually the first clinical signs of toxicosis to appear, frequently resulting in dehydration
symptoms.onset: Within 1 day (or delayed up to 2–4 days)

symptoms.name: Lethargy, Weakness, and Depression
symptoms.notes: These symptoms emerge as a secondary response to systemic hypoxia caused by the profound loss of oxygen-carrying red blood cells
symptoms.onset: After a lag of several days (typically 3–5 days)

symptoms.name: Pale or Icteric Mucous Membranes (Jaundice)
symptoms.notes: Pale gums indicate a lack of oxygenated blood (anemia), while jaundice (yellowing of the skin/mucosa) indicates severe red blood cell destruction leading to hyperbilirubinemia
symptoms.onset: After a lag of several days (typically 3–5 days)

symptoms.name: Tachypnea, Dyspnea, and Cyanosis
symptoms.notes: This is a compensatory response to systemic tissue hypoxia and methemoglobinemia, with cyanosis indicating a life-threatening lack of oxygen in the blood

symptoms.name: Tachycardia and Heart Murmur
symptoms.notes: Tachycardia develops as the heart pumps faster to compensate for impaired oxygen transportation45. In one documented feline case, a grade IV heart murmur was also observed upon physical examination

symptoms.name: Hemoglobinuria (Dark Urine)
symptoms.notes: This is a hallmark sign of severe poisoning caused by damaged red blood cells rupturing and releasing hemoglobin, which is then excreted through the kidneys3.... This process can lead to secondary acute kidney injury known as hemoglobinuric nephrosis

symptoms.name: Hypothermia and Cold Sensitivity
symptoms.notes: Affected cats may present with an abnormally low body temperature, measured as low as 36.6 °C in one clinical case

symptoms.name: Collapse and Death
symptoms.notes: Death occurs when oxidative damage to the blood, severe hemolytic anemia, and resulting tissue hypoxia are extensive and overwhelm the animal's bodily systems
symptoms.onset: Within 24 hours (with massive doses) or several days after ingestion

treatments.description: of the procedure: Providing the cat with supplemental oxygen and transfusing whole blood to restore the body's depleted oxygen-carrying capacity
treatments.name: 1. Oxygen Therapy and Blood Transfusions (Life-Saving Stabilization)
treatments.notes: This is the most urgent priority for severely affected cats presenting with life-threatening signs such as respiratory distress, systemic hypoxia (from methemoglobinemia), or profound hemolytic anemia (where the hematocrit drops below 15–20%)2.... Because there is no specific antidote for onion toxi

treatments.description: of the procedure: Administering an emetic drug, typically an α 2 ​ -adrenergic agonist like dexmedetomidine (via intramuscular or intravenous injection), to force the cat to expel the ingested plant material before toxins are absorbed
treatments.name: Induction of Emesis (Early Gastrointestinal Decontamination)
treatments.notes: This is highly valuable but only indicated if the ingestion was very recent (usually caught within 1 to 3 hours) and the cat is currently asymptomatic1.... Crucially, 3% hydrogen peroxide must never be used to induce vomiting in cats, as it is strictly contraindicated and can cause fatal hemorrhagic

treatments.description: of the procedure: Administering oral activated charcoal (typically 1 to 4 g/kg) to bind to and adsorb any residual toxins remaining in the stomach and intestines
treatments.name: Administration of Activated Charcoal (Secondary Decontamination)
treatments.notes: This is typically administered directly after emesis to trap toxins before they enter the bloodstream1.... However, it should be noted that its actual efficacy in binding to and preventing the production of Allium-derived sulfides is still unknown

treatments.description: of the procedure: Hospitalizing the cat to administer continuous IV fluids to rehydrate the animal, support blood pressure, and maintain proper renal perfusion
treatments.name: Intravenous (IV) Fluid Therapy (Systemic Supportive Care)
treatments.notes: This treatment is indicated if the cat has suffered extensive fluid loss from severe vomiting and diarrhea, or presents with hypotension1. Furthermore, it is an essential protective measure if the cat develops hemoglobinuria (dark urine caused by ruptured red blood cells clearing through the urinary

treatments.description: of the procedure: Administering anti-sickness/anti-nausea medications, stomach protectants, and sometimes antioxidant vitamins (C and E) or N-acetyl-cysteine
treatments.name: Gastrointestinal and Antioxidant Medications (Symptomatic Relief)
treatments.notes: GI protectants and anti-sickness medications are used to manage mild-to-moderate abdominal pain, vomiting, and diarrhea4. While antioxidants are sometimes administered in an attempt to combat the oxidative damage to red blood cells, they have shown minimal proven protective effects against onion tox
